The classical name was "Misatodokoro" (みさとづかさ){{ref|万葉集 16・3859}}. The Chinese-style name included terms like {{ill|Jingzhao|en|Jingzhao|WD=Q10883119|lt=Jingzhaofu}}, {{ill|Fengyi|ja|馮翊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fengyi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 左馮翊|1=Pingyi (region)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11057388}}, and {{ill|Fufeng|ja|扶風|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fufeng|qq=}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the alternate term "Kyōshoku" (京職) referred to the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|en|Kyoto Shoshidai|WD=Q855977|lt=Kyoto Shoshidai}}.
Kyōshiki divided the capital into "Sakyō" (左京) and "Ukyō" (右京), with separate administrative offices, '''Sakyōshiki''' and '''Ukyōshiki''', respectively. Their chiefs were titled '''Sakyō no Daibu''' (左京大夫) and '''Ukyō no Daibu''' (右京大夫).
In {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}}, the offices were situated north of {{ill|Anekoji Street|ja|姉小路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anekoji Street|qq=}} along the {{ill|Suzaku Avenue|en|Suzaku Avenue|WD=Q2378654|lt=Suzaku Avenue}}.
== Functions == The Kyōshiki managed the administration, judiciary, and policing within the capital. It first appeared in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. The Kyōshiki's responsibilities were comparable to those of {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=provincial governors (Kokushi)}}, but its focus was on the artificial urban environment of the capital, a key part of state operations. Unlike the Kokushi, who were {{ill|local officials|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/local officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}}, the Kyōshiki were {{ill|central officials|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/central officials|qq=}}, highlighting its distinct role.
For example, while both managed {{ill|family registers|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family registers|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, the Kyōshiki handled specific capital-related functions, such as marketplaces, measurement standards, and bridge repairs. Unlike provinces where population management and local taxes were critical, the capital was governed as a hub of imperial administration. Over time, discrepancies between the register and reality grew, leading to reforms like granting Kyōshiki the power to adjust registers in {{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 18 (876 CE).{{ref|市川 2009, P128-140}}
Kyōshiki had jurisdiction over marketplaces through subordinate offices like {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}}: Sakyōshiki managed the Eastern Market, and Ukyōshiki oversaw the Western Market. Regional responsibilities were handled by '''Bōryō''' (坊令) and '''Bōchō''' (坊長), analogous to provincial officials but selected from local residents. However, with urban growth during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, noble households and their retainers increasingly ignored Kyōshiki orders. In response, the {{ill|Bōryō system|ja|坊令制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōryō system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was replaced by local figures such as {{ill|Hōtoneri|ja|保刀禰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoneri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebiishi|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi|qq=}} assumed policing functions.{{ref|市川 2009, P103-113}}
== Structure == The Kyōshiki included two offices for the eastern and western parts of the capital: * '''Daibu''' (大夫): Chief officer ({{ill|Shōgoi|ja|正五位上|1=Senior Fifth Rank|lt=Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正五位}} → {{ill|Jushii|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jushii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}}) * '''Suke''' (亮): Deputy officer ({{ill|Jugoi|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jugoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}) * '''Taijō''' (大進): Senior officer ({{ill|Juroku|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Juroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}}) * '''Shōshin''' (少進): Junior officer ({{ill|Shichii|ja|正七位上|1=Senior Seventh Rank|lt=Senior Seventh Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Seventh Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正七位}}) * '''Daisakan''' (大属): Senior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|正八位下|1=Senior Eighth Rank|lt=Senior Eighth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位}}) * '''Shōsakan''' (少属): Junior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|従八位上|1=Junior Eighth Rank|lt=Junior Eighth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従八位}}) * '''Bōryō''' (坊令): Neighborhood head (unranked → {{ill|Shoshōi|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoshōi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}) ** '''Bōchō''' (坊長): Subordinate neighborhood official * Support staff included '''Shishō''' (clerks), '''Shokushō''' (operational staff), and laborers.
The administrative sub-offices included: * {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}} (Eastern and Western Market Offices)
== Comparison with Tang China == Unlike Japan, Tang China managed its capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an}} through regular provincial systems like {{ill|Kuannei Dao|ja|関内道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuannei Dao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which supervised {{ill|Yongzhou (ancient China)|en|Yongzhou (ancient China)|WD=Q8015641|lt=Yongzhou}} under its jurisdiction. Additionally, tasks like policing and tax collection were divided among multiple layers of officials. The Kyōshiki's centralized model reflected Japan's adaptation of Tang principles to a smaller, more urbanized environment.{{ref|市川 2009, P216-264}}
== Kyōshiki in the Muromachi and Sengoku Periods == In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} held the title of '''Ukyō no Daibu''' for generations, leading to the nickname "Kyōchōke" (京兆家). During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the title of '''Sakyō no Daibu''' became a popular honorary title for {{ill|daimyō|ja|戦国大名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daimyō|1=Sengoku daimyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5384684}}, including prominent clans like the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda}} and {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Japanese administrative system|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} * {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebishō|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebishō|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|京職|version=100841430|comment=Imported full ja history}}

However, with urban growth during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, noble households and their retainers increasingly ignored Kyōshiki orders. In response, the {{ill|Bōryō system|ja|坊令制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōryō system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was replaced by local figures such as {{ill|Hōtoneri|ja|保刀禰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoneri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebiishi|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi|qq=}} assumed policing functions.{{ref|市川 2009, P103-113}}

Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正五位}} → {{ill|Jushii|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jushii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}}) * '''Suke''' (亮): Deputy officer ({{ill|Jugoi|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jugoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}) * '''Taijō''' (大進): Senior officer ({{ill|Juroku|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jugoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}) * '''Taijō''' (大進): Senior officer ({{ill|Juroku|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Juroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}}) * '''Shōshin''' (少進): Junior officer ({{ill|Shichii|ja|正七位上|1=Senior Seventh Rank|lt=Senior Seventh Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Seventh Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正七位}}) * '''Daisakan''' (大属): Senior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|正八位下|1=Senior Eighth Rank|lt=Senior Eighth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位}}) * '''Shōsakan''' (少属): Junior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|従八位上|1=Junior Eighth Rank|lt=Junior Eighth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従八位}}) * '''Bōryō''' (坊令): Neighborhood head (unranked → {{ill|Shoshōi|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoshōi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}) ** '''Bōchō''' (坊長): Subordinate neighborhood official * Support staff included '''Shishō''' (clerks), '''Shokushō''' (operational staff), and laborers.
The administrative sub-offices included: * {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}} (Eastern and Western Market Offices)
== Comparison with Tang China == Unlike Japan, Tang China managed its capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an}} through regular provincial systems like {{ill|Kuannei Dao|ja|関内道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuannei Dao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which supervised {{ill|Yongzhou (ancient China)|en|Yongzhou (ancient China)|WD=Q8015641|lt=Yongzhou}} under its jurisdiction. Additionally, tasks like policing and tax collection were divided among multiple layers of officials. The Kyōshiki's centralized model reflected Japan's adaptation of Tang principles to a smaller, more urbanized environment.{{ref|市川 2009, P216-264}}
== Kyōshiki in the Muromachi and Sengoku Periods == In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} held the title of '''Ukyō no Daibu''' for generations, leading to the nickname "Kyōchōke" (京兆家). During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the title of '''Sakyō no Daibu''' became a popular honorary title for {{ill|daimyō|ja|戦国大名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daimyō|1=Sengoku daimyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5384684}}, including prominent clans like the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda}} and {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Japanese administrative system|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} * {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebishō|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebishō|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|京職|version=100841430|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--Right Capital Ward Officer--><!--Right Capital Ward Officer--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:京職]] {{wikidata link|Q11374719}} [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Kyoto in the Heian period]] [[Category:Officials of the Ritsuryō System]] [[Category:Public security systems]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:京職]][[zh:京職]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:京職]][[zh:京職]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:京職]][[zh:京職]]
The '''Kyōshiki''' was an {{ill|Executive (government)|en|Executive (government)|WD=Q35798|lt=administrative office}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} in Japan, responsible for overseeing judicial, administrative, and policing functions within the {{ill|Capital city|en|Capital city|WD=Q5119|lt=capital}}. The classical name was "Misatodokoro" (みさとづかさ){{ref|万葉集 16・3859}}. The Chinese-style name included terms like {{ill|Jingzhao|en|Jingzhao|WD=Q10883119|lt=Jingzhaofu}}, {{ill|Fengyi|ja|馮翊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fengyi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 左馮翊|1=Pingyi (region)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11057388}}, and {{ill|Fufeng|ja|扶風|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fufeng|qq=}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the alternate term "Kyōshoku" (京職) referred to the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|en|Kyoto Shoshidai|WD=Q855977|lt=Kyoto Shoshidai}}.
Kyōshiki divided the capital into "Sakyō" (左京) and "Ukyō" (右京), with separate administrative offices, '''Sakyōshiki''' and '''Ukyōshiki''', respectively. Their chiefs were titled '''Sakyō no Daibu''' (左京大夫) and '''Ukyō no Daibu''' (右京大夫).
In {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}}, the offices were situated north of {{ill|Anekoji Street|ja|姉小路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anekoji Street|qq=}} along the {{ill|Suzaku Avenue|en|Suzaku Avenue|WD=Q2378654|lt=Suzaku Avenue}}.
== Functions == The Kyōshiki managed the administration, judiciary, and policing within the capital. It first appeared in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. The Kyōshiki's responsibilities were comparable to those of {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=provincial governors (Kokushi)}}, but its focus was on the artificial urban environment of the capital, a key part of state operations. Unlike the Kokushi, who were {{ill|local officials|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/local officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}}, the Kyōshiki were {{ill|central officials|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/central officials|qq=}}, highlighting its distinct role.
For example, while both managed {{ill|family registers|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family registers|1=koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, the Kyōshiki handled specific capital-related functions, such as marketplaces, measurement standards, and bridge repairs. Unlike provinces where population management and local taxes were critical, the capital was governed as a hub of imperial administration. Over time, discrepancies between the register and reality grew, leading to reforms like granting Kyōshiki the power to adjust registers in {{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan}} 18 (876 CE).{{ref|市川 2009, P128-140}}
Kyōshiki had jurisdiction over marketplaces through subordinate offices like {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}}: Sakyōshiki managed the Eastern Market, and Ukyōshiki oversaw the Western Market. Regional responsibilities were handled by '''Bōryō''' (坊令) and '''Bōchō''' (坊長), analogous to provincial officials but selected from local residents. However, with urban growth during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, noble households and their retainers increasingly ignored Kyōshiki orders. In response, the {{ill|Bōryō system|ja|坊令制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōryō system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was replaced by local figures such as {{ill|Hōtoneri|ja|保刀禰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoneri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebiishi|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi|qq=}} assumed policing functions.{{ref|市川 2009, P103-113}}
== Structure == The Kyōshiki included two offices for the eastern and western parts of the capital: * '''Daibu''' (大夫): Chief officer ({{ill|Shōgoi|ja|正五位上|1=Senior Fifth Rank|lt=Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正五位}} → {{ill|Jushii|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jushii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}}) * '''Suke''' (亮): Deputy officer ({{ill|Jugoi|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jugoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}) * '''Taijō''' (大進): Senior officer ({{ill|Juroku|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Juroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}}) * '''Shōshin''' (少進): Junior officer ({{ill|Shichii|ja|正七位上|1=Senior Seventh Rank|lt=Senior Seventh Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Seventh Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正七位}}) * '''Daisakan''' (大属): Senior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|正八位下|1=Senior Eighth Rank|lt=Senior Eighth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位}}) * '''Shōsakan''' (少属): Junior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|従八位上|1=Junior Eighth Rank|lt=Junior Eighth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従八位}}) * '''Bōryō''' (坊令): Neighborhood head (unranked → {{ill|Shoshōi|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoshōi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}) ** '''Bōchō''' (坊長): Subordinate neighborhood official * Support staff included '''Shishō''' (clerks), '''Shokushō''' (operational staff), and laborers.
The administrative sub-offices included: * {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}} (Eastern and Western Market Offices)
== Comparison with Tang China == Unlike Japan, Tang China managed its capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an}} through regular provincial systems like {{ill|Kuannei Dao|ja|関内道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuannei Dao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which supervised {{ill|Yongzhou (ancient China)|en|Yongzhou (ancient China)|WD=Q8015641|lt=Yongzhou}} under its jurisdiction. Additionally, tasks like policing and tax collection were divided among multiple layers of officials. The Kyōshiki's centralized model reflected Japan's adaptation of Tang principles to a smaller, more urbanized environment.{{ref|市川 2009, P216-264}}
== Kyōshiki in the Muromachi and Sengoku Periods == In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan}} held the title of '''Ukyō no Daibu''' for generations, leading to the nickname "Kyōchōke" (京兆家). During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the title of '''Sakyō no Daibu''' became a popular honorary title for {{ill|daimyō|ja|戦国大名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daimyō|1=Sengoku daimyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5384684}}, including prominent clans like the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda}} and {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Japanese administrative system|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} * {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebishō|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebishō|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|京職|version=100841430|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The '''Kyōshiki''' was an {{ill|Executive (government)|en|Executive (government)|WD=Q35798|lt=administrative office|1=Executive (government)}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} in Japan, responsible for overseeing judicial, administrative, and policing functions within the {{ill|Capital city|en|Capital city|WD=Q5119|lt=capital|1=Capital city}}. The classical name was "Misatodokoro" (みさとづかさ){{ref|万葉集 16・3859}}. The Chinese-style name included terms like {{ill|Jingzhao|en|Jingzhao|WD=Q10883119|lt=Jingzhaofu|1=Jingzhao}}, {{ill|Fengyi|ja|馮翊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fengyi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 左馮翊|1=Pingyi (region)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11057388}}, and {{ill|Fufeng|ja|扶風|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fufeng|qq=}}. During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the alternate term "Kyōshoku" (京職) referred to the {{ill|Kyoto Shoshidai|en|Kyoto Shoshidai|WD=Q855977|lt=Kyoto Shoshidai|1=Kyoto Shoshidai}}.
Kyōshiki divided the capital into "Sakyō" (左京) and "Ukyō" (右京), with separate administrative offices, '''Sakyōshiki''' and '''Ukyōshiki''', respectively. Their chiefs were titled '''Sakyō no Daibu''' (左京大夫) and '''Ukyō no Daibu''' (右京大夫).
In {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}}, the offices were situated north of {{ill|Anekoji Street|ja|姉小路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anekoji Street|qq=}} along the {{ill|Suzaku Avenue|en|Suzaku Avenue|WD=Q2378654|lt=Suzaku Avenue|1=Suzaku Avenue}}.
== Functions == The Kyōshiki managed the administration, judiciary, and policing within the capital. It first appeared in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. The Kyōshiki's responsibilities were comparable to those of {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=provincial governors (Kokushi)|1=Kokushi (official)}}, but its focus was on the artificial urban environment of the capital, a key part of state operations. Unlike the Kokushi, who were {{ill|local officials|ja|外官|1=Gaikan|lt=Local officials (Gaikan)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/local officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Gaikan|qq=}}, the Kyōshiki were {{ill|central officials|ja|京官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/central officials|qq=}}, highlighting its distinct role.
For example, while both managed {{ill|family registers|ja|古代日本の戸籍制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/family registers|1=Koseki system in classical Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410913}}, the Kyōshiki handled specific capital-related functions, such as marketplaces, measurement standards, and bridge repairs. Unlike provinces where population management and local taxes were critical, the capital was governed as a hub of imperial administration. Over time, discrepancies between the register and reality grew, leading to reforms like granting Kyōshiki the power to adjust registers in {{ill|Jōgan|en|Jōgan|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} 18 (876 CE).{{ref|市川 2009, P128-140}}
Kyōshiki had jurisdiction over marketplaces through subordinate offices like {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}}: Sakyōshiki managed the Eastern Market, and Ukyōshiki oversaw the Western Market. Regional responsibilities were handled by '''Bōryō''' (坊令) and '''Bōchō''' (坊長), analogous to provincial officials but selected from local residents. However, with urban growth during the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, noble households and their retainers increasingly ignored Kyōshiki orders. In response, the {{ill|Bōryō system|ja|坊令制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bōryō system|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was replaced by local figures such as {{ill|Hōtoneri|ja|保刀禰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoneri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebiishi|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebiishi|qq=}} assumed policing functions.{{ref|市川 2009, P103-113}}
== Structure == The Kyōshiki included two offices for the eastern and western parts of the capital: * '''Daibu''' (大夫): Chief officer ({{ill|Shōgoi|ja|正五位上|1=Senior Fifth Rank|lt=Senior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōgoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正五位}} → {{ill|Jushii|ja|従四位下|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade|1=Junior Fourth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jushii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}}) * '''Suke''' (亮): Deputy officer ({{ill|Jugoi|ja|従五位下|1=Junior Fifth Rank|lt=Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jugoi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従五位}}) * '''Taijō''' (大進): Senior officer ({{ill|Juroku|ja|従六位下|1=Junior Sixth Rank|lt=Junior Sixth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Juroku|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Sixth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従六位}}) * '''Shōshin''' (少進): Junior officer ({{ill|Shichii|ja|正七位上|1=Senior Seventh Rank|lt=Senior Seventh Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shichii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Seventh Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正七位}}) * '''Daisakan''' (大属): Senior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|正八位下|1=Senior Eighth Rank|lt=Senior Eighth Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位}}) * '''Shōsakan''' (少属): Junior secretary ({{ill|Hachii|ja|従八位上|1=Junior Eighth Rank|lt=Junior Eighth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachii|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Eighth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従八位}}) * '''Bōryō''' (坊令): Neighborhood head (unranked → {{ill|Shoshōi|ja|少初位下|1=Junior Initial Rank|lt=Junior Initial Rank, Lower Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoshōi|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Initial Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 少初位}}) ** '''Bōchō''' (坊長): Subordinate neighborhood official * Support staff included '''Shishō''' (clerks), '''Shokushō''' (operational staff), and laborers.
The administrative sub-offices included: * {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichinotsukasa|qq=}} (Eastern and Western Market Offices)
== Comparison with Tang China == Unlike Japan, Tang China managed its capital {{ill|Chang'an|en|Chang'an|WD=Q6501000|lt=Chang'an|1=Chang'an}} through regular provincial systems like {{ill|Kuannei Dao|ja|関内道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuannei Dao|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which supervised {{ill|Yongzhou (ancient China)|en|Yongzhou (ancient China)|WD=Q8015641|lt=Yongzhou|1=Yongzhou (ancient China)}} under its jurisdiction. Additionally, tasks like policing and tax collection were divided among multiple layers of officials. The Kyōshiki's centralized model reflected Japan's adaptation of Tang principles to a smaller, more urbanized environment.{{ref|市川 2009, P216-264}}
== Kyōshiki in the Muromachi and Sengoku Periods == In the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the {{ill|Hosokawa clan|en|Hosokawa clan|WD=Q2736120|lt=Hosokawa clan|1=Hosokawa clan}} held the title of '''Ukyō no Daibu''' for generations, leading to the nickname "Kyōchōke" (京兆家). During the {{ill|Sengoku period|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, the title of '''Sakyō no Daibu''' became a popular honorary title for {{ill|daimyō|ja|戦国大名|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/daimyō|1=Sengoku daimyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5384684}}, including prominent clans like the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|WD=Q1210114|lt=Takeda|1=Takeda clan}} and {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō|1=Later Hōjō clan}}.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Japanese administrative system|ja|日本の官制|1=structure of the ritsuryō government|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese administrative system|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8193883}} * {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} * {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|lt=Office of Police and Justice|lt=Kebishō|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kebishō|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|京職|version=100841430|comment=Imported full ja history}}
